content.text: Production GenAI systems do not stay at the architecture they launched with. The model improves, the team grows, the user base scales, and each of these changes exposes gaps in the current architecture that a new design must address. The key discipline is evolving the architecture incrementally — in response to observed operational signals — rather than designing the target-state platform upfront and trying to build to it.

attrs.alt: GenAI architecture evolution stages: Monolith → Modular → Composable → Platform, each with fitness strengths and weaknesses, and specific operational triggers for transition

content.text: The Case Against Big-Bang Redesigns
The temptation after a successful launch is to redesign the architecture toward what the team believes the mature system should look like. This produces two failure modes:
The redesign takes 3–6 months, during which the production system receives no improvements and technical debt accumulates
The new architecture is designed for scale that does not yet exist, adding complexity that the current team cannot maintain

Evolutionary architecture avoids both failures by making each architectural change small, motivated by a specific operational signal, and validated against fitness functions before it becomes the production baseline.
Principle:
Every architectural change must address a specific, observed problem — not a hypothetical future problem. "We might need this" is not a trigger; "we measured this" is.
Fitness Functions as Architectural Guards

An evolutionary architecture uses fitness functions — automated checks that verify the architecture continues to satisfy its quality requirements — to prevent one improvement from degrading another property.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class FitnessFunction:
    name: str
    description: str
    check: Callable[[], tuple[bool, str]]  # returns (passed, message)
    blocking: bool                          # if True, fails the architecture change

def check_eval_regression() -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Fitness function: eval score must not drop > 2% from baseline.
    Blocks any architectural change that degrades quality.
    """
    import httpx
    result = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/eval/latest").json()
    baseline = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/eval/baseline").json()
    delta = result["avg_score"] - baseline["avg_score"]
    if delta < -0.02:
        return False, f"Eval score dropped {-delta:.1%} from baseline (threshold: 2%)"
    return True, f"Eval score delta: {delta:+.1%} (within threshold)"

def check_p99_latency() -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Fitness function: p99 latency must stay within 10% of pre-change baseline.
    """
    import httpx
    current = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/metrics/p99/current").json()["ms"]
    baseline = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/metrics/p99/baseline").json()["ms"]
    ratio = current / baseline
    if ratio > 1.10:
        return False, f"p99 latency {current}ms is {ratio:.0%} of baseline {baseline}ms (threshold: 110%)"
    return True, f"p99 latency {current}ms ({ratio:.0%} of baseline)"

def check_cost_per_request() -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Fitness function: cost per request must not increase > 5%.
    """
    import httpx
    current = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/cost/per-request/current").json()["usd"]
    baseline = httpx.get("https://internal.ops/cost/per-request/baseline").json()["usd"]
    ratio = current / baseline
    if ratio > 1.05:
        return False, f"Cost per request ${current:.4f} is {ratio:.0%} of baseline (threshold: 105%)"
    return True, f"Cost per request ${current:.4f} ({ratio:.0%} of baseline)"

ARCHITECTURE_FITNESS_FUNCTIONS = [
    FitnessFunction("eval_regression", "Eval score must not drop > 2%", check_eval_regression, blocking=True),
    FitnessFunction("latency_regression", "p99 latency within 10% of baseline", check_p99_latency, blocking=True),
    FitnessFunction("cost_regression", "Cost per request within 5% of baseline", check_cost_per_request, blocking=False),
]

def run_fitness_checks(functions: list[FitnessFunction]) -> bool:
    all_passed = True
    for fn in functions:
        passed, message = fn.check()
        status = "PASS" if passed else ("FAIL (blocking)" if fn.blocking else "WARN")
        print(f"  [{status}] {fn.name}: {message}")
        if not passed and fn.blocking:
            all_passed = False
    return all_passed

Fitness functions run as CI checks on every architectural change. They make the non-regression contract explicit: you can change the architecture however you want, as long as these measured properties stay within their bounds.
Stage 1 to Stage 2: Extracting Observability and Eval
The first architectural evolution almost always involves two changes that are often bundled together because they share the same prerequisite: structured logging.
Adding Structured Logging

import json, time
from contextlib import contextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class InferenceTrace:
    trace_id: str
    model: str
    prompt_version: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    latency_ms: float
    finish_reason: str
    cost_usd: float
    user_id: str | None = None
    session_id: str | None = None
    error: str | None = None

@contextmanager
def trace_inference(trace_id: str, model: str, prompt_version: str):
    """
    Context manager that emits a structured trace log for every LLM call.
    Drop-in replacement for untraced direct API calls.
    """
    start = time.monotonic()
    trace = InferenceTrace(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        model=model,
        prompt_version=prompt_version,
        input_tokens=0,
        output_tokens=0,
        latency_ms=0,
        finish_reason="",
        cost_usd=0.0,
    )
    try:
        yield trace
    except Exception as e:
        trace.error = str(e)
        raise
    finally:
        trace.latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
        print(json.dumps({"type": "inference_trace", **asdict(trace)}))

# Migration: before
# response = client.messages.create(...)

# Migration: after (adds observability without changing behavior)
# with trace_inference(trace_id, model, prompt_version) as trace:
#     response = client.messages.create(...)
#     trace.input_tokens = response.usage.input_tokens
#     trace.output_tokens = response.usage.output_tokens
#     trace.finish_reason = response.stop_reason

This extraction is purely additive: the existing inference logic does not change. The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'trace_inference', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  context manager wraps the existing call and emits a structured log line. After one week of structured logging, the team has the data needed to build an eval pipeline from production samples.
Adding a Prompt Version Registry
Moving from inline prompts to a versioned prompt registry is the change that enables safe iteration. It decouples prompt deployment from code deployment.

import json, os
from functools import lru_cache

class PromptRegistry:
    """
    Minimal prompt registry that reads versioned prompt files from disk.
    Replaces hardcoded f-strings with versioned, cacheable prompt templates.
    """
    def __init__(self, prompt_dir: str = "prompts"):
        self.prompt_dir = prompt_dir

    @lru_cache(maxsize=32)
    def get(self, name: str, version: str = "current") -> str:
        """
        Loads prompt from prompts/<name>/<version>.txt.
        'current' is a symlink to the active version.
        """
        path = os.path.join(self.prompt_dir, name, f"{version}.txt")
        with open(path) as f:
            return f.read()

    def list_versions(self, name: str) -> list[str]:
        prompt_path = os.path.join(self.prompt_dir, name)
        return [f[:-4] for f in os.listdir(prompt_path) if f.endswith(".txt")]

registry = PromptRegistry()

# Before: hardcoded in application code
# system_prompt = "You are a helpful document assistant. Today is {date}..."

# After: loaded from versioned registry
system_prompt = registry.get("document-qa", version="v1.3")
Stage 2 to Stage 3: Adding Canary Deployment

The trigger for moving from Stage 2 to Stage 3 is typically the first time a model upgrade or prompt change causes a regression that was not caught before it reached all users. Canary deployment addresses this by routing a small fraction of traffic to the new version before full rollout.

import hashlib
from typing import Literal

ModelVersion = Literal["v1", "v2"]

class CanaryRouter:
    """
    Routes requests to canary vs. stable model versions based on user ID hash.
    Canary percentage is controlled by configuration, not code deployment.
    """
    def __init__(self, canary_pct: float = 0.05):
        self.canary_pct = canary_pct    # 5% of traffic

    def route(self, user_id: str) -> ModelVersion:
        """
        Deterministic routing: same user always gets the same version,
        preventing inconsistent experience for returning users.
        """
        hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
        bucket = (hash_val % 100) / 100
        return "v2" if bucket < self.canary_pct else "v1"

router = CanaryRouter(canary_pct=0.05)

def get_model_for_request(user_id: str) -> str:
    version = router.route(user_id)
    model_map = {
        "v1": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
        "v2": "claude-opus-4-8",   # canary: testing new model
    }
    return model_map[version]

The key constraint is  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'deterministic routing', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} : a user assigned to the canary cohort at 9am must still be in the canary cohort at 3pm. Hash-based routing on user ID guarantees this without requiring session storage.
Shadow Mode for Risk-Free Evaluation

Shadow mode is a variant of canary deployment where the new version receives a copy of all traffic but its responses are not returned to users. This allows evaluation of quality and cost without any user-facing risk.

import asyncio
import httpx

async def shadow_inference(
    request: dict,
    stable_endpoint: str,
    shadow_endpoint: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Sends the request to both endpoints concurrently.
    Returns only the stable response; logs the shadow response for comparison.
    """
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        stable_task = client.post(stable_endpoint, json=request, timeout=30)
        shadow_task = client.post(shadow_endpoint, json=request, timeout=30)

        stable_resp, shadow_resp = await asyncio.gather(
            stable_task, shadow_task, return_exceptions=True
        )

    # Log shadow result for offline comparison — never return it to the user
    if not isinstance(shadow_resp, Exception):
        import json
        print(json.dumps({
            "type": "shadow_comparison",
            "request_id": request.get("id"),
            "stable_latency_ms": stable_resp.elapsed.total_seconds() * 1000,
            "shadow_latency_ms": shadow_resp.elapsed.total_seconds() * 1000,
            "stable_tokens": stable_resp.json().get("usage", {}).get("total_tokens"),
            "shadow_tokens": shadow_resp.json().get("usage", {}).get("total_tokens"),
        }))

    # Return only the stable response
    if isinstance(stable_resp, Exception):
        raise stable_resp
    return stable_resp.json()
Stage 3 to Stage 4: Building the AI Gateway

The transition to Stage 4 is triggered by organizational scale, not technical scale. When three or more product teams are each building their own LLM call management, rate limit handling, cost tracking, and prompt versioning, the duplication cost exceeds the cost of building a centralized gateway.
What the Gateway Centralizes
Capability
Without gateway
With gateway
Rate limit handling
Each team implements retry/backoff independently
Single implementation, consistent behavior, shared quota pool
Cost attribution
Token costs aggregated at the org level only
Per-team, per-feature, per-user cost attribution with chargeback
Prompt versioning
Each team maintains its own registry
Central registry with access control, audit log, rollback
Safety guardrails
Each team applies independently (or not at all)
Enforced at the gateway — no team can bypass
Observability
Per-team dashboards with incompatible schemas
Unified trace schema, cross-team comparison possible
Model routing
Each team hardcodes provider/model choices
Gateway routes by cost, latency, capability automatically

The gateway does not appear at Stage 1, 2, or 3 because its cost (a dedicated platform team, a centralized service, governance overhead) exceeds its benefit when a single team is building a single product. The trigger is specifically organizational, not technical.
Architectural Decision Records for AI Systems
Every non-trivial architectural change should be documented in an Architectural Decision Record (ADR). For AI systems, ADRs have additional fields that conventional software ADRs do not include.

# ADR-2026-007: Introduce Canary Deployment for Prompt Changes

**Date:** 2026-06-15
**Status:** Accepted
**Authors:** @eng-lead, @sre-lead

## Context
The June 2026 incident (INC-2026-0609) was caused by a prompt change that
degraded table extraction quality for ~20% of enterprise queries. The change
passed CI eval (which lacked coverage for multi-page tables) and was deployed
to 100% of traffic simultaneously. Discovery was via support escalation
(48h after deploy).

## Decision
Introduce a canary routing layer that routes 5% of traffic to the new prompt
version before full rollout. The canary requires:
- 1-hour soak at 5%: no SLO breaches and no increase in support tickets
- Production eval on the canary cohort: score must be within 2% of stable
- Manual approval from the feature lead before full rollout

## Alternatives considered
1. Longer pre-deploy eval suite: rejected — cannot cover all production query
   distributions without a live traffic sample
2. Feature flags per user: rejected — adds complexity without the production
   traffic sample that canary provides

## Fitness functions enforced by this change
- eval_regression (blocking): canary eval must not drop > 2% from stable
- latency_regression (blocking): canary p99 must stay within 10% of stable
- support_ticket_volume (advisory): monitor for spike during soak period

## GenAI-specific considerations
- Canary routing must be deterministic (hash-based on user_id) to prevent
  users experiencing inconsistent behavior within a session
- Shadow mode (0% user exposure) should precede canary for high-risk changes
  (new model versions, RAG strategy changes)

## Consequences
- Adds ~30 minutes to the prompt deployment cycle (soak + approval)
- Requires SRE on-call awareness during canary soak periods
- Eliminates the class of regression that caused INC-2026-0609
When NOT to Evolve the Architecture
Architecture changes have costs: migration effort, learning curve, increased complexity. Several signals indicate that an architectural change is premature:
Proposed change
Premature if...
Wait for...
Add AI gateway
Team size < 3, single product
3+ teams building on same stack
Introduce multi-provider routing
Current provider meets SLO
SLO breach attributed to provider outage
Build self-serve eval platform
Fewer than 5 eval runs per week
Eval cycle is the bottleneck for shipping
Add fine-tuning pipeline
Prompt engineering still has headroom
Prompt-only improvements hit a measurable ceiling
Federated observability
Single deployment environment
Multiple deployment regions or teams with incompatible schemas

The anti-pattern is architecture-driven development: building infrastructure because it is technically interesting, not because an operational signal demands it. Every architectural layer costs ongoing maintenance — it must earn its place by solving a measured problem.
Summary
Incremental architecture evolution for AI systems keeps the system aligned with its current operational maturity:
Four stages
— Monolith → Modular → Composable → Platform; each stage has a specific fitness profile and specific operational triggers for transition
Fitness functions
— automated checks that verify eval score, latency, and cost do not regress across architectural changes
Stage 1→2
— structured logging and prompt versioning are always the first extractions; they enable everything else
Stage 2→3
— canary deployment prevents regression from reaching all users; shadow mode validates before any user exposure
Stage 3→4
— triggered by organizational scale, not technical scale; the AI gateway centralizes rate limits, cost attribution, and guardrails
ADRs with AI fields
— document the operational trigger, fitness functions enforced, and GenAI-specific considerations for each change
No premature evolution
— build the next stage when an operational signal demands it, not when the architecture looks interesting
